title: Reasons for Richness & Poorness of Countries 2/3

transcript.text: They'll let the beginnings of North America were different from the beginnings of South America.
The beginnings of South America were from Spain.
Spain was a wannabe Roman Empire.
They grew their empire the same way that the Romans did, by conquering and just taking from the people they conquered.
They took all the gold from the Mexicans.
They took all the Aztecs' ornaments, melted it down to gold, took it home.
It got really rich that way, the richest country in the world back in the late 1600s.
That's how they could build a Spanish Armada and try to attack England.
But that kind of an empire is out of date.
It was what you could call, compared to the British Empire, I think an evil empire.
And certainly compared to our modern-day American empire, that was an evil empire.
They had these, back in the early 1400s, back in like the mid 1400s, you had King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella, who were just King and Queen of the fiefdom of Spain.
Spain wasn't a country, it was just a bunch of fiefdoms.
And what happened is, they were crooks, they were like mafia, mafiosos.
They took over the whole country by borrowing all of their money from borrowing all the money off of the Jews who lived there.
And they used that money to attack all the other fiefdoms and take control of the country.
And then once they had control of the country, they didn't want to have to pay the Jews back, so they created the Spanish Inquisition.
And they kicked all the Jews out, so all the Jews had to go to Ireland and Scotland.
And then they kicked out the Moors, who were the Muslims in the south.
And so then they had control, complete control over Spain.
And they got control in a very evil way.
They, the Pope in Italy, disapproved of them.
And they even had a war with the Pope of naval battle off the coast of Italy, which they won, because they had more money.
And then they decided they were going to go ahead and kind of have their Spanish Empire, just like the Romans had their heyday.
And so they, Christopher Columbus gave them the idea to go to America.
And Isabella was a Catholic, and so she wanted to spread her Catholicism.
And certainly, I think that they did believe that they, that they were going to do good by spreading their Catholicism.
And, but she was also kind of lying to themselves, because I think the real reason was so that they could go out and loot.
So they went to, they went to America, they looted, they looted Mexico, they looted, they got the silver, huge silver mines out of Bolivia, and looted the Aztecs.
They had a lot of gold, and made all their money, became the most powerful, richest country in Europe.
And attacked England, and then they lost their armada, and then they kind of went down since then.
But they still had, the Spanish still had an empire up until like 1910, when we took Cuba from them.
So, so when they brought their people over to America, they were bringing, it was more of a, it was more of a state, kind of like a state sponsored exodus of people.
It wasn't, it was sponsored by the Spanish country, the Spanish crown, for the purposes of, of making money.
So they would just hire people, they would pay people to go over there.
Like there was their job to go over there.
Um, and I think most people didn't really have a reason to go over there.
So they, they were just, I think they got the poor people to go over to America.
Um, just individually.
Um, just like, uh, taking kids off the street who didn't have families and they took over them, took them over there and they, so they didn't go over there with their families.
They went over there individually and so they didn't have this cohesive unit when they first started out in America.
And they had this very structured society down there.
Um, you had, uh, uh, with the Haciendas, you would have the, uh, the guy who, if he was Spanish, if he was born in Spain, then he was number one honcho.
And then people who had Spanish parents, uh, they were number two honcho.

And then if you had, and then, and then it went just incrementally down, down from there, the more Spanish blood you have, the higher up you are, the more connections you have with Spain, the higher up you are.
And then, uh, and then if you were like an Indian, you just kind of, you were just basically a slave.
And, uh, so it's very stratified.
So it was a stratified society from the very beginning.
And, which kind of gives people a lack of hope if it's stratified like that.
Uh, the poor people kind of just do what they're told and don't really, uh, think that they have any way of ever moving up through the ranks.
And then the people who are up on top have disdain for those down below because they're told that they're better than them and superior to them.
And, um, and so, and you said, and then also they, they were, it was like a state sponsored religion.
They're all Catholic.
So they're all told what to do.
It was this big religion.
They were told what to believe and everything from the, from the Pope way back on the other side of the earth.
And, uh, so that's even more of a reason not to think for yourself.
So, uh, whereas with North America, the people who came to North America came, um, they paid their own way to come to America.
They built their own boats.
They came over with their families, mother and father and child hand in hand that came here.
They created their own little enclaves.

It was like each colony was its own country.
And then they grew and then they, so it was people who knew how to, uh, who had a vision and who knew how to unite.
And, uh, we were much more effective in, uh, killing off the Indians.

And, uh, so fast forward to today, South America.
